(9TH CIR.2000) 214 F.3D 1058; ROES 1-2 V. SFBSC MGMT., LLC, 77 F.SUPP.3D 990 (N.D. CAL. 2015). THERE IS GOOD CAUSE TO GRANT PLAINTIFF'S MOTION BECAUSE: (1) THERE IS A SIGNIFICANT SOCIAL STIGMA ASSOCIATED WITH NUDE/SEMI-NUDE DANCING; (2) THERE ARE RISKS ASSOCIATED WITH THE JOB, INCLUDING CUSTOMERS DISCOVERING THE DANCER'S TRUE NAME OR ADDRESS; (3) PLAINTIFF WOULD BE HESITANT TO MAINTAIN THIS ACTION IF HER NAME WERE DISCLOSED BECAUSE OF FUTURE JOB PROSPECTS; AND (4) PLAINTIFF SEEKS TO PROTECT HER PRIVACY RIGHTS. THESE INTERESTS OUTWEIGH THE PREJUDICE TO DEFENDANT AND THE PUBLIC'S INTEREST IN KNOWING PLAINTIFF'S IDENTITY. ORDER SIGNED. JUDGE: ERNEST GOLDSMITH. CLERK: ROSIE NOGUERA. Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Allegations From Plaintiffs Second Amended Complaint
Off Calendar Jun-22-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: APPEARANCES - ATTY. STEVEN G. TIDRICK OF THE TIDRICK LAW FIRM, TEL. NO. 1-510-788-5100 FOR THE PLAINTIFF BY TELEPHONE APPEARANCE;
ATTY. SHANE M. CAHILL OF LONG & LEVIT LLP, TEL. NO. 1-415-438-4594 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT.

RULING - A R G U E D; THE COURT MODIFIED ITS TENTATIVE RULING AS FOLLOWS: Defendant Heritage Management Service, Inc.'s motion to strike certain allegations from the second amended complaint is granted in part and denied in part. Paragraph 54 is stricken without leave to amend. Plaintiff cannot file an amended PAGA notice that resurrects time-barred claims. The parties agree that a 1-year statute of limitations applies. Plaintiff must serve a PAGA notice within one year of the alleged violation. (Soto v. Castlerock Farming and Transport Inc. (E.D. Cal. 4/6/12) 2012 WL 1292519). Paragraph 1 of plaintiff's federal complaint shows that plaintiff stopped working for defendant by 10/30/14. Thus, any new claims that plaintiff asserts in the 11/19/15 amended PAGA notice are untimely. The relation back doctrine does not apply to the amended PAGA notice. (Wilson v. People By and Through Dept. of Public Works, (1969) 271 Cal.App.2d 665, 669). Some of the claims that defendant characterizes as "new" in the amended PAGA notice were sufficiently identified in the original PAGA notice while others were not. The motion to strike paragraphs 66-68 and 71. 81-83, 88-89, and 91 is denied. The motion to strike Labor Code 558 allegations is denied per the reasoning of Stoddart v. Express Servs., (E.D. Cal. 9/16/15) 2015 WL 5522142. The original PAGA notice (i.e. paragraph 83 of the federal complaint) referred to Labor Code 510. This same analysis applies to (1) the Labor Code 1197.1 allegations because the second cause of action in the federal complaint cited Labor Code 1197 and alleged a failure to pay all wages; (2) the Labor Code 1194.2 [minimum wage liquidated damages] allegations because the federal complaint referred to Labor Code 1194 [failure to pay wages] in the second cause of action; and (3) the Labor Code sec. 226.3 [penalty for failure to provide itemized statement] allegations because the federal complaint referred to Labor Code 226 [itemized statements] at paragraphs 85 and 86. Paragraphs 58-63 and 110-113 are stricken without leave to amend. The Labor Code 226.8 [willful misclassification] allegations are stricken because the federal complaint did not cite section 226.8 or a related provision. The Labor Code 1174 [duty to maintain employee records] and 1174.5 [penalty for failing to maintain records] allegations are stricken because the original PAGA notice did not reference these sections or anything related to them. The motion to strike paragraphs 75 and 83 is granted which refers to 1194 seeking wages. Plaintiff can recover wages as a penalty under Flowers v. Los Angeles County Metropolitan Transportation Authority (2015) 243 Cal.App.4th 66, 86. The cited portions of paragraphs 70, 79, and 87 are stricken without leave to amend. Plaintiff cannot bring a PAGA action to directly enforce a wage order per Flowers. The portions of paragraph 120 referring to Labor Code 226.8, 1174, and 1174.5 and the IWC orders are stricken without leave to amend. The motion is denied as to the remainder of that paragraph. Prevailing party to prepare a form of order and to comply with CRC 3.1312. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Ann Delgado, CSR # 11185 302/hk

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Allow Plaintiff To Proceed With This Action Using The Pseudonym Jane Roe And For A Protective Order
Off Calendar Jan-21-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel or Interested Person.

The Court adopted its tentative ruling. PLAINTIFF JANE ROE'S Motion To Allow Plaintiff To Proceed With This Action Using The Pseudonym Jane Roe And For A Protective Order is granted. The use of pseudonyms is proper to protect against harassment, injury, ridicule, personal embarrassment, or social stigmatization. See Does I thru XXIII v. Advanced Textile Corp. (9th Cir.2000) 214 F.3d 1058; Roes 1-2 v. SFBSC Mgmt., LLC, 77 F.Supp.3d 990 (N.D. Cal. 2015). There is good cause to grant plaintiff's motion because: (1) there is a significant social stigma associated with nude/semi-nude dancing; (2) there are risks associated with the job, including customers discovering the dancer's true name or address; (3) plaintiff would be hesitant to maintain this action if her name were disclosed because of future job prospects; and (4) plaintiff seeks to protect her privacy rights. These interests outweigh the prejudice to defendant and the public's interest in knowing plaintiff's identity. Order signed.

Judge: Ernest Goldsmith. Clerk: Rosie Noguera. Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976. (302/EG)
